title: AI’s economic gains proving elusive: Altman
full_text:
OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman says the promised economic gains from artificial intelligence are proving elusive, warning that the technology is turning out to be “maybe a lot harder” to translate into real-world impact than the industry had anticipated.
Speaking at the Commonwealth Bank Accelerate AI event, Mr Altman told CBA chief executive Matt Comyn he was bracing for more changes to the jobs market as a result of the new technology.
Mr Altman said AI was going to be positive and negative for the jobs market, but the outcome was “likely to be very different than we thought”.
“I don’t think we’re going to have the kind of jobs apocalypse that some of the companies in our space advocate or talk about,” Mr Altman said.
But Mr Altman, who appeared via videolink, said a raft of new changes for coding and technology were “about to happen again”, after his firm’s coding systems shook global jobs markets six months ago.
“I thought that there was going to be more impact on entry-level white-collar jobs being eliminated by now,” he said. “I think I understand more about why that wasn’t done, obviously gratefully, but that is an area where my intuitions were just off.” Mr Altman said he did not expect the breadth of changes kicked off by OpenAI’s coding programs earlier this year, which had resulted in many firms seeking to replace coders with its AI agents.
Recently, WiseTech and Afterpay owner Block announced massive headcount cuts, citing AI as a key factor.
CBA has also directly linked redundancies to AI, announcing plans to sack 45 staff after introducing new AI agents. The bank is in negotiation with the Finance Sector Union over a new wage deal amid concern from staff that more cuts are to come as AI finds root at the bank.
Mr Altman said OpenAI faced pushback from security teams at the time it launched the coding tools. But in the wake of OpenAI’s coding launch, things had clearly shifted.
“Truly one of the most rapid adoptions of a new technology at a serious enterprise level, with serious upsides and downsides, has taken place,” he said.
OpenAI, one of the world’s frontrunner AI firms, signed a deal with CBA to supply its suite of AI tools to the banking major in August last year.
This has led to CBA rolling out its products across the bank, alongside other AI offerings.
On Tuesday, CBA announced that it was preparing to release two new AI-powered agents to its retail and business banking customers, powered by a combination of Anthropic and OpenAI.
Mr Altman said he recently tried to let AI write his personal messages and emails, but said this was “surprisingly dehumanising”.
“We really do care about people and our interactions with people,” he said. “We have not yet figured out how to have a world where people and AI are collaborating.” Mr Altman said Australia had the potential to be a “data centre capital of the world”, with the country blessed by natural resources and energy.
He said Australia was “in the very top few places in the world” to be a data centre player.
“Demand for sufficient high-quality and sufficiently low-cost intelligence is effectively uncapped. Australia also has stable institutions, predictable rule of law, a great national security posture and very strong partners.” Mr Comyn said CBA could also see the value in Australia hosting data centres and AI firms but said labour shortages and planning controls could frustrate this. “Energy will be a factor clearly as it relates to training modes; there’s an issue around copyright and trying to come up with a solution that respects those rights,” he said.
Mr Comyn said there should be a general principle governing AI firms around the use of copyrighted material, including that they should make a “fair economic contribution” in the countries they operate.
“The rights of holders of intellectual property should be respected, and you’ve got to think through what’s the most appropriate sort of commercial response to that,” he said.
Major media companies have called for the government to keep copyright protections in place, amid a call from parts of the tech sector to weaken rights to allow AI companies to train models on Australian content. Mr Comyn said he was also concerned about the potential for AI to enable crime after the bank was recently revealed to have written $1bn in home loans to borrowers, some of whom had supplied AI-generated documents.
He said business had to grapple with how information was sourced or how misinformation was created, and said AI was already being used to organise crowds and influence thinking.
CBA and other companies had to think about how to use it defensively as well as internally.
"It's not a choice to not build this capability because it’s going to be used against you in a variety of different ways,” he said.
Coles CEO Leah Weckert said the supermarket she led was using AI systems to make 109 billion predictions a day, with this ensuring that products arrived at its stores when needed.
She said the supermarket operator had been using AI for 10 years, and it now used AI to respond to changes in the weather and the school year, which influenced customer behaviour. It would “not be very far into the future” when AI would be able “to do your shopping for you with no interference from you”.
